revenue_by_merchant.sql:
SELECT m.merchant_name,
       SUM(t.amount) as total_revenue,
       COUNT(*) as txn_count
FROM fact_transactions t, dim_merchant m
WHERE t.merchant_id = m.merchant_id
AND t.transaction_date > '2024-01-01'
GROUP BY m.merchant_name
ORDER BY total_revenue
LIMIT 10;

issues.title: Missing filter for completed transactions
issues.problem: The query does not filter transactions by status 'COMPLETED' to calculate revenue.
issues.fix: Add AND t.status = 'COMPLETED' to the WHERE clause.

issues.title: Implicit join syntax
issues.problem: The query uses an implicit join syntax which is less readable.
issues.fix: Replace the implicit join with an explicit INNER JOIN.

issues.title: Lack of comments
issues.problem: The query lacks comments explaining the logic, which can be helpful for future maintainers.
issues.fix: Add comments to explain the purpose of each clause.

review.corrected_sql: SELECT m.merchant_name, SUM(t.amount) as total_revenue, COUNT(*) as txn_count FROM fact_transactions t INNER JOIN dim_merchant m ON t.merchant_id = m.merchant_id WHERE t.transaction_date > '2024-01-01' AND t.status = 'COMPLETED' GROUP BY m.merchant_name ORDER BY total_revenue DESC LIMIT 10;
review.summary: The query has a critical correctness issue by not filtering for completed transactions and uses an implicit join syntax. It also lacks comments for readability.

customer_spend.sql:
SELECT c.customer_name, c.email, c.tier,
       SUM(t.amount) as lifetime_value,
       COUNT(t.transaction_id) as total_orders
FROM dim_customer c
LEFT JOIN fact_transactions t ON c.customer_id = t.customer_id
WHERE t.status = 'COMPLETED'
GROUP BY c.customer_name, c.email
HAVING SUM(t.amount) > 1000
ORDER BY lifetime_value DESC;

issues.title: Incorrect lifetime_value calculation
issues.problem: The query is summing the amount for all transactions, not just completed ones, which violates the business rule.
issues.fix: SUM(CASE WHEN t.status = 'COMPLETED' THEN t.amount ELSE 0 END) as lifetime_value

issues.title: Missing filter for 'COMPLETED' status
issues.problem: The WHERE clause is filtering on the joined table, which can exclude NULL values from the LEFT JOIN.
issues.fix: WHERE t.status = 'COMPLETED' OR t.status IS NULL

issues.title: Lack of comments
issues.problem: The query lacks comments explaining the logic, which makes it harder to understand.
issues.fix: Add comments to explain the purpose of each clause.

issues.title: Inconsistent naming
issues.problem: The aliases 'lifetime_value' and 'total_orders' could be more consistent with business terminology.

review.corrected_sql: SELECT c.customer_name, c.email, c.tier, SUM(CASE WHEN t.status = 'COMPLETED' THEN t.amount ELSE 0 END) as total_revenue, COUNT(CASE WHEN t.status = 'COMPLETED' THEN t.transaction_id ELSE NULL END) as total_orders FROM dim_customer c LEFT JOIN fact_transactions t ON c.customer_id = t.customer_id WHERE t.status = 'COMPLETED' OR t.status IS NULL GROUP BY c.customer_name, c.email HAVING SUM(CASE WHEN t.status = 'COMPLETED' THEN t.amount ELSE 0 END) > 1000 ORDER BY total_revenue DESC;
review.summary: The query had a critical correctness issue with the lifetime value calculation and a readability issue with naming. It also lacked comments and had a minor readability issue with inconsistent naming.

daily_failure_rate.sql:
SELECT transaction_date,
       (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM fact_transactions f2
        WHERE f2.status = 'FAILED'
        AND f2.transaction_date = t.transaction_date) as failed_count,
       COUNT(*) as total_count,
       failed_count / total_count * 100 as failure_rate
FROM fact_transactions t
GROUP BY transaction_date
ORDER BY transaction_date;

issues.title: Incorrect calculation of failure rate
issues.problem: The failure rate calculation is incorrect because it uses the alias 'failed_count' and 'total_count' which are not recognized in the SELECT clause.

issues.title: Correlated subquery
issues.line: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM fact_transactions f2 WHERE f2.status = 'FAILED' AND f2.transaction_date = t.transaction_date
issues.problem: The correlated subquery can be very inefficient as it runs for each row in the outer query.
issues.fix: Use a JOIN to avoid the correlated subquery.

issues.title: Lack of comments
issues.problem: The query lacks comments explaining the logic, which makes it harder to understand.
issues.fix: Add comments to explain the logic.

review.corrected_sql:
WITH failed_transactions AS (SELECT transaction_date, COUNT(*) as failed_count FROM fact_transactions WHERE status = 'FAILED' GROUP BY transaction_date)
SELECT t.transaction_date,
       f.failed_count,
       COUNT(*) as total_count,
       COALESCE(f.failed_count, 0) / COUNT(*) * 100 as failure_rate
FROM fact_transactions t
LEFT JOIN failed_transactions f ON t.transaction_date = f.transaction_date
GROUP BY t.transaction_date, f.failed_count
ORDER BY t.transaction_date;
review.summary: The query had issues with correctness in failure rate calculation, performance due to a correlated subquery, and readability due to lack of comments. The corrected query uses a JOIN to improve performance and includes comments for better readability.
